2/12
INTRODUCTION

Introduction to Decision Trees

Decision trees are a type of machine learning model. They learn a hierarchy of if/else questions.

1/10
SLIDE 04

Theory and Core Concepts

Decision trees learn a hierarchy of if/else questions, leading to a decision. They are used for classification and regression tasks. Essentially, they ask questions like 'Is feature i larger than value a?' for continuous data.

  • Decision trees learn a hierarchy of if/else questions📄 p.84
  • Used for classification and regression tasks📄 p.84
  • Ask questions like 'Is feature i larger than value a?'📄 p.85
📄 Page 84-85
2/10
SLIDE 05

Implementation and Examples

The visualization of the tree provides a great in-depth view of how the algorithm makes predictions. A decision tree can be built based on a newly created dataset. For example, a decision tree to distinguish among several animals can be created.

  • Visualization of the tree provides insight into predictions📄 p.91
  • Decision tree can be built on a newly created dataset📄 p.98
  • Example: decision tree to distinguish among animals📄 p.85
📄 Page 85, 91, 98
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-22. A decision tree to distinguish among several animals — Page 85
3/10
SLIDE 06

Parameters and Limitations

Decision trees tend to overfit the training data. Random forests are one way to address this issue. They build multiple decision trees on different subsets of the data and combine their predictions.

  • Decision trees tend to overfit the training data📄 p.97
  • Random forests address overfitting📄 p.97
  • Random forests combine multiple decision trees📄 p.97
📄 Page 97
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-32. Comparison of predictions made by a linear model and predictions made — Page 96
4/10
SLIDE 07

Ensembles of Decision Trees: Theory

Ensembles of decision trees, such as random forests, build multiple decision trees on different subsets of the data. They combine the predictions of these trees to produce a more accurate prediction.

  • Random forests build multiple decision trees📄 p.97
  • Combine predictions of multiple trees📄 p.97
  • Produce a more accurate prediction📄 p.97
📄 Page 97
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-53. Decision functions learned with the same parameters but different random — Page 128
5/10
SLIDE 08

Ensembles of Decision Trees: Implementation

The algorithm for building an ensemble of decision trees is slightly modified from the standard decision tree algorithm. It builds multiple decision trees on different subsets of the data and combines their predictions.

  • Modified algorithm for building ensembles📄 p.98
  • Builds multiple decision trees on different subsets📄 p.98
  • Combines predictions of multiple trees📄 p.98
📄 Page 98
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-28. Feature importances computed from a decision tree learned on the Breast — Page 92
6/10
SLIDE 09

Ensembles of Decision Trees: Parameters and Tradeoffs

The main downside of decision trees is that they tend to overfit and provide poor generalization. Random forests can address this issue by building multiple decision trees and combining their predictions.

  • Decision trees tend to overfit📄 p.97
  • Random forests address overfitting📄 p.97
  • Combine multiple decision trees📄 p.97
📄 Page 97
7/10
SLIDE 10

Decision Trees and Feature Importances

Decision trees and decision tree-based models provide a feature_importances_ attribute, which can be used to select the most important features.

  • Decision trees provide feature_importances_📄 p.252
  • Feature_importances_ can be used for feature selection📄 p.252
  • Select the most important features📄 p.252
📄 Page 252
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-23. Two-moons dataset on which the decision tree will be built — Page 86
8/10
SLIDE 11

Binning, Discretization, Linear Models, and Trees

The best way to represent data depends on the problem. Binning, discretization, linear models, and trees are all used for different purposes.

  • Binning, discretization, linear models, and trees are used for different purposes📄 p.234
  • Best representation of data depends on the problem📄 p.234
  • Different models are suited for different tasks📄 p.234
📄 Page 234
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 4-1. Comparing linear regression and a decision tree on the wave dataset — Page 235
9/10
✦

Final Thoughts

Decision trees and their ensembles are powerful tools for machine learning tasks. They provide a simple and interpretable way to make predictions.

Decision trees
10/10
